messages.content: In an alternate reality, the islands of Southeast Asia became the homes and melting pot for ancient cultures and civilizations, from the Ancient Egyptians, Carthaginians, Athenians, Spartans, Romans, Aksumites, Scandinavian Vikings, Feudal Japanese, and Yuan Dynasty Mongols, as well as the many pre-colonial indigenous peoples that lived there, welcoming them with open arms. These ancient cultures were preserved here and integrated and assimilated with the pre-colonial Southeast Asians. Today, a seafaring unit launches from what's now known today as the Philippines, stopping at pre-colonial Hawaii and eventually the Aztec Empire. And while any conflict was avoided with even the Romans, Japanese and Mongols trading steel weapons, armor and gunpowder, lives were lost, albeit willingly. It seems that the Vikings and Spartans overhearing the Aztecs's human sacrifice traditions caught their interest. Vikings seeking honorable death and Spartans being devout and religious, wished to become human sacrifices for the Aztecs, as a sign of respect to their religions and hopes that their deaths may improve the Aztecs's crops and societies.

However, when news of this reached the rest of the seafaring unit, many were shocked and outraged. The idea of sacrificing oneself to please another culture was unthinkable to them. The unit was divided, with some wanting to honor their fallen comrades' wishes, and others vehemently against it.

Ultimately, it was decided that the sacrifice of the Vikings and Spartans would not be allowed. It was seen as a betrayal of their own beliefs and an unnecessary loss of life. The two cultures were mourned and honored in other ways, but live sacrifices were not an option.

This event led to discussions amongst the seafaring unit about the importance of respecting other cultures, but also the importance of staying true to one's own beliefs and values. It became a reminder that cultural exchange and understanding should always come from a place of mutual respect and agreement, rather than blindly adopting another culture's practices.

The idea of sacrifice was denied, with even a majority of the Aztecs believing that it wasn't even necessary. And while the Spartans and Vikings would soon get their honorable deaths in battle in the future, it was not going to be here in Tenochtitlan. The Aztecs however, while thankful for the iron weapons, armor and gunpowder, wished to join the seafaring unit back to their islands and see the world outside theirs, the seafaring units happily agreed.

The Aztecs were fascinated by the different cultures they encountered on their journey, and they were welcomed with open arms by the people they met. They shared their own culture and traditions, and in turn, learned about the customs and beliefs of others.

As they traveled, the Aztecs began to see the world in a new light. They realized that their own way of life was just one of many, and that there was much to learn from other cultures. They also saw the importance of preserving their own traditions and history, and worked to ensure that their culture would continue to thrive.

When they returned to their homeland, the Aztecs brought with them new ideas and perspectives. They shared their experiences with their people, and encouraged them to embrace the diversity of the world around them. They also continued to trade with the seafaring unit, exchanging goods and ideas that enriched both cultures.

In the end, the journey of the seafaring unit and the Aztecs was a testament to the power of cultural exchange and understanding. It showed that even in a world of different beliefs and traditions, people could come together and learn from one another, creating a brighter and more harmonious future for all.

The seafaring unit stops at the Inca Empire, doing the same setting up of diplomacy and trade. Some Incas wish to join them. As the seafaring unit of these cultures return to the Southeast Asian islands, the Aztecs and Incas that have accompanied them are confused by one small thing: these cultures happily traded steel weapons, armor, gunpowder and even horses, yet were more happy with simple crops as the traded items. They ask how something as simple as an ear of maize or potatoes could be more valuable to them. The pre-colonial Southeast Asians, Ancient Egyptians, Carthaginians, Athenians, Spartans, Romans, Aksumites, Vikings, Japanese, Mongols and Muslim and Hindu converts laugh, explaining to them why crops and livestock serve a more positive purpose for peace and progression than just weapons, armor and horses.

The Southeast Asians explain that while weapons and armor may be useful in times of war, they are not the key to a prosperous society. Crops and livestock, on the other hand, are essential for sustaining life and building a strong economy. They are the foundation of civilization, providing food, clothing, and other basic necessities.

The Aztecs and Incas begin to see the wisdom in this, and they start to appreciate the value of agriculture. They learn about the different crops and farming techniques used in Southeast Asia, and they begin to incorporate these practices into their own societies.

As a result, the Aztecs and Incas are able to improve their own agricultural output, leading to greater prosperity and stability. They also continue to trade with the seafaring unit, exchanging goods and ideas that benefit all parties involved.

In the end, the journey of the seafaring unit and the Aztecs and Incas that joined them was a testament to the power of cultural exchange and understanding. It showed that even in a world of different beliefs and traditions, people could come together and learn from one another, creating a brighter and more harmonious future for all.

Though the seafaring unit continues to make stops at pre-colonial Hawaii, pre-colonial New Zealand and pre-colonial Australia, the Aztecs and Incas, as well as the Native Hawaiians, Māori and Aboriginal Australians who joined them, are surprised by this melting pot of civilizations and cultures. The pre-colonial peoples of both Borneo, Sumatra, Java and the Philippines have impressively kept their own traditions and cultures, whilst also having communities of their cultures mix with the others. It was common to find a supposed pre-colonial Filipino or Malaysian who had the blood of Ancient Egyptians or Romans. The new peoples are fascinated by this and make settlement without need of invasion or colonization or war. And as the western seafaring unit returns to the islands, having made contact with the Mali and Songhai Empires, as well as the Somali and Swahili city-states, the pre-colonial Southeast Asians explode with progress. Unknown to them, Christopher Columbus had landed in the Americas, none the wiser of the previous encounters.

The pre-colonial Southeast Asians continue to thrive, building upon the knowledge and experiences gained from their interactions with other cultures. They continue to trade and exchange ideas with the seafaring unit, as well as with the new peoples who have settled in their lands.

As they grow and progress, the pre-colonial Southeast Asians become a beacon of hope and inspiration for other cultures around the world. They show that it is possible to maintain one's own traditions and beliefs, while also embracing the diversity of the world around them.

Meanwhile, the seafaring unit continues to explore and make contact with new cultures and civilizations. They learn about the rich history and traditions of the Mali and Songhai Empires, as well as the Somali and Swahili city-states. They continue to trade and exchange ideas, building bridges between different peoples and cultures.

As for Christopher Columbus, his arrival in the Americas would eventually lead to the colonization and exploitation of the indigenous peoples there. But in this alternate reality, the pre-colonial Southeast Asians serve as a reminder that cultural exchange and understanding can lead to progress and prosperity, without the need for invasion or colonization.

As the years passed and the multicultural pre-colonial Southeast Asian islands continue to progress significantly, the battle of Tenochtitlan progresses, and though the Aztecs lose, the Spanish suffer more losses in the alternate universe. Hernan Cortés notices that the Aztecs they fought also had horses, steel weapons and armor alongside their own native weapons and armor, and gunpowder used for proto-bombs and small cannons. Cortés is unsure as to how this is possible, never knowing fully of the of the seafaring units that visited the Aztec Empire years earlier. Meanwhile in Borneo, the pre-colonial peoples and other cultures celebrate as the Aztecs who settled have built their own pyramids, with the Athenian and Roman architects adding a Greco-Roman temple on top, as well as Chinese additions thanks to the Mongols and Chinese from the newly established Ming Dynasty.

The celebration in Borneo is a testament to the power of cultural exchange and understanding. The Aztecs, Athenians, Romans, Mongols, and Chinese have all contributed their own unique perspectives and skills to create something truly remarkable. The pre-colonial peoples of Borneo have embraced this diversity, and have worked together to create something that is greater than the sum of its parts.

Meanwhile, in the Americas, the Spanish continue to struggle against the Aztecs, who are now better equipped to defend themselves. The Spanish are surprised by the Aztecs' use of horses, steel weapons and armor, and gunpowder, and they begin to realize that they are facing a formidable opponent.

As the battle of Tenochtitlan rages on, the Spanish begin to question their own tactics and strategies. They realize that they cannot simply rely on their superior technology and weaponry to win the war. They must also learn to understand and respect the culture and traditions of the Aztecs, and work to find a way to coexist peacefully.

In the end, the battle of Tenochtitlan is a turning point in the history of the Americas. It marks the beginning of a new era of cultural exchange and understanding, where different peoples and cultures can come together and learn from one another, creating a brighter and more harmonious future for all.

Back in the Southeast Asian islands, gliding was invented early, due to the abundance of resources and information. Though aerodynamics haven't been fully understood yet, the logic they followed was making something similar to bird wings that was far larger than a human, and didn't require flapping. Chinese and Japanese Pagodas, Mesoamerican pyramids, minarets from Muslim missionaries building mosques and Egyptian obelisks were built in the pre-colonial Southeast Asian islands, perfect to test out gliding without relying on mountains and cliffs.

The invention of gliding in the pre-colonial Southeast Asian islands is a remarkable achievement. The people there have used their resources and knowledge to create something that was previously thought impossible. The construction of Chinese and Japanese Pagodas, Mesoamerican pyramids, minarets from Muslim missionaries building mosques, and Egyptian obelisks is a testament to the diversity of cultures and traditions that have come together in this region.

As gliding becomes more popular, people begin to experiment with different designs and techniques. They learn about aerodynamics and the principles of flight, and they work to improve their gliders. They also begin to use gliders for practical purposes, such as transportation and reconnaissance.

The invention of gliding in the pre-colonial Southeast Asian islands is a significant milestone in the history of aviation. It shows that even without the benefit of modern technology, people can achieve great things through ingenuity and perseverance. It also serves as a reminder of the importance of cultural exchange and understanding, and how different peoples and cultures can come together to create something truly remarkable.

One of the Aztec immigrants, a first generation immigrant, married a Japanese woman and have children in pre-colonial Luzon.  As their children grow, his sons choose to join the military. Thanks to the many settlers, his children learn of Mongol mounted archery and how to dismount their horse and immediately climb up a Carthaginian war elephant, Spartan phalanx and Roman testudo, using Japanese bows and katanas and his father's own macuahuitl, even the newest innovation: Short and long lantaka from the Yuan Mongols (essentially early firearms).

The children of the Aztec immigrant and Japanese woman are a testament to the power of cultural exchange and understanding. They have inherited the traditions and skills of their parents, as well as the knowledge and techniques of the many cultures that have settled in the pre-colonial Southeast Asian islands.

As they join the military, they bring with them a unique set of skills and perspectives. They are able to combine the techniques of Mongol mounted archery, Carthaginian war elephants, Spartan phalanx, Roman testudo, Japanese bows and katanas, and the macuahuitl of their Aztec heritage, as well as the short and long lantaka from the Yuan Mongols.

Their skills and knowledge prove to be invaluable in battle, and they quickly rise through the ranks. They become respected leaders and strategists, admired for their ability to adapt and innovate in the face of adversity.

The children of the Aztec immigrant and Japanese woman are a shining example of the potential that lies in cultural exchange and understanding. They show that by embracing diversity and learning from one another, we can achieve great things and create a brighter future for all.

Today in 1521, Magellan and his expedition fleet land in the pre-colonial Philippines, only to be shocked by what appeared to be a thriving civilization of cultures both old and new. He and his conquistadors notice cultures that should've gone extinct, such as the Romans, Ancient Egyptians, Spartans and Athenians, as well as cultures he didn't know but knew enough weren't native to these islands, such as the Aksumites, Vikings, Native Hawaiians, Japanese, Aztecs and Incas. Even Christianization seems to have been something he can't do, as the Aksumites and Vikings brought Coptic Orthodox AND Roman Catholic Christianity to the islands. And while the Battle of Mactan plays out the same, in this alternate universe, Ferdinand Magellan survives and returns to Spain. Meanwhile, the multicultural group manage to capture one of Magellan's galleons that were abandoned, and begin reverse engineering their ship and leftover muskets. To the multicultural groups's surprised, it seems as though before the Battle of Mactan began, some Spanish conquistadors chose to abandon Magellan early in favor of the hospitality of the islands and advanced medicinal practices, choosing to ally themselves.

The arrival of Magellan and his expedition fleet in the pre-colonial Philippines is a turning point in the history of the region. The multicultural group that has settled there is surprised by the arrival of the Spanish, but they are also intrigued by the new technology and ideas that they bring with them.

As they capture one of Magellan's galleons and begin to reverse engineer it, they realize the potential that this technology holds. They work tirelessly to understand the workings of the ship and the muskets, and they begin to incorporate these innovations into their own societies.

Meanwhile, Magellan is surprised by the thriving civilization that he finds in the pre-colonial Philippines. He realizes that the people there are not the primitive savages that he had expected, but rather a complex and sophisticated society with its own unique traditions and cultures.

As he returns to Spain, he brings with him stories of the wonders that he has seen in the pre-colonial Philippines. He speaks of the advanced medicinal practices, the thriving trade networks, and the diverse cultures that have come together in this region.

The arrival of Magellan and his expedition fleet marks the beginning of a new era of cultural exchange and understanding in the pre-colonial Philippines. It shows that even in a world of different beliefs and traditions, people can come together and learn from one another, creating a brighter and more harmonious future for all.

As word spreads of how islands in Southeast Asia managed to become the home of many cultures from the past, rising colonial powers from France, England and Portugal find opportunities. Surely these islands had more wealth than initially thought. If what Magellan said was true, they could find common ground knowing some of those people have adopted Christianity. Others seek to legitimize their power and rule, hoping to ally themselves with the Romans living in the islands, thus declaring themselves as the legitimate heirs to the Roman Empire. The nations and kingdoms present in Scandinavia at the time also get word of Magellan's voyage. The Vikings were shrouded in legend and mystery, and knowing those islands have numerous Vikings could hold promise for negotiation and a better understanding of their heritage. As all this was going on, with the help of the Conquistadors who stayed and the workmanship of the group, galleons were built and their firearms improved further.

The arrival of colonial powers from France, England, and Portugal in the pre-colonial Southeast Asian islands is a cause for concern for the multicultural group that has settled there. They fear that these new arrivals will seek to exploit their resources and subjugate their people.

However, they also see the potential for cultural exchange and understanding. They realize that these colonial powers may bring with them new ideas and technologies that could benefit their societies.

As negotiations begin, the multicultural group works to protect their interests and maintain their independence. They are wary of the intentions of these new arrivals, but they are also open to the possibility of cooperation and mutual benefit.

Meanwhile, the Vikings living in the pre-colonial Southeast Asian islands become a source of fascination for the new arrivals. They are shrouded in legend and mystery, and their presence in this region is a testament to the diversity of cultures that have come together here.

As the galleons are built and firearms are improved, the multicultural group becomes more confident in their ability to defend themselves and their way of life. They continue to work towards progress and prosperity, while also maintaining their traditions and cultures.

The arrival of colonial powers in the pre-colonial Southeast Asian islands marks a new chapter in the history of the region. It shows that even in a world of different beliefs and traditions, people can come together and learn from one another, creating a brighter and more harmonious future for all.
